figures.caption: Fig. 2: SAL (higher = smoother) and TED (lower = smoother) for Open Drawer from [14]. Scores correlate with policy success across modalities.
Fig. 4: SAL violin plots across five metric variants and three modalities for three tasks from [14]. Kinesthetic teaching consistently produces higher (smoother) SAL.

Fig. 1: Smoothness metrics applied to synthetic trajectories. Left to right: 3D trajectory, individual axes, SAL spectral analysis, and TED Bézier curve fitting. Smooth trajectories (top) have steep spectral decay and small Bézier residuals; noisy trajectories (bottom) show flat spectra and large deviations.
Fig. 5: SAL distributions stratified by operator-reported difficulty for Push Block (top) and Pick and Place (bottom). Easier trajectories have higher SAL (smoother).
TABLE II: RoboMimic filtering results (% success). K demos selected from |D| by TED or SAL vs. full data. mh = multi-human, ph = proficient-human, b–b = better–better.
